raw_note:
Doctor: Hello, I understand you have a history of close contact with dogs and sheep. Can you tell me about any symptoms you've been experiencing recently?
Patient: Yes, for over a month now, I've had diminished vision in my left eye, especially when chewing. But I haven't had any other concomitant symptoms.
Doctor: I see. And I noticed that you were diagnosed with hepatic CE back in 2010. Did you receive any treatment for that?
Patient: No, I didn't receive any treatment at the time.
Doctor: Alright, let's discuss your recent test results. Your magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT) of the paranasal sinus revealed a 47 x 44-mm cystic mass in your left infratemporal fossa, which has been determined as type CE2 according to the WHO classification.
Patient: Oh, that doesn't sound good.
Doctor: Some lesions have infiltrated into the intracranial and orbital areas, and the upper maxillary sinus cavity is compressed, as evidenced by the contrast-enhanced MRI.
Patient: What else did you find, doctor?
Doctor: A transthoracic echocardiogram showed a rounded cyst measuring 16 x 20 mm with a clear boundary, regular shape, and homogenous hypoecho in your left ventricle myocardium. Your left ventricle systolic function is normal, with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 66%.
Patient: Alright, and what about the rest of the tests?
Doctor: The contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed a low-density mass measuring approximately 33.05 x 12.27 mm within your left ventricle. Your upper abdominal CT scan revealed two round, low-density, echogenic cystic lesions involving segment seven of your liver, with the largest lesions measuring approximately 28.09 x 27.32 mm.
Patient: That's a lot to take in.
Doctor: Yes, and we also detected multiple small vesicular structures within the lesions, determined to be WHO type CE2. Small lesions were also visible beside the large ones.
Patient: Anything else, doctor?
Doctor: We found multiple purely unilocular cystic low-attenuation masses in your abdomen, with the largest lesions being type CE1 and measuring approximately 60.44 x 54.40 mm. There is also a low-attenuation multiseptated mass of type CE2 in your pancreatic tail, measuring approximately 44.96 x 51.43 mm.
Patient: What about the pelvic CT scan?
Doctor: The pelvic CT scan showed an uneven distribution of intracystic density and the presence of WHO type CE3B daughter cysts, measuring 92.02 x 37.21 mm.
Patient: I see. What's the next step, doctor?
Doctor: Given your diagnosis and the extent of the cysts, we need to discuss a treatment plan for you. I'll consult with a team of specialists and schedule a follow-up appointment to go over our recommendations. In the meantime, please monitor your symptoms and let us know if anything changes.
Patient: Okay, thank you, doctor.
soap:
S: The patient reports diminished vision in the left eye, particularly when chewing, persisting for over a month. No other concomitant symptoms were mentioned. The patient has a history of close contact with dogs and sheep and was diagnosed with hepatic cystic echinococcosis (CE) in 2010 but did not receive treatment.
O: MRI and CT scans of the paranasal sinus show a 47 x 44-mm cystic mass in the left infratemporal fossa, classified as WHO type CE2, with infiltration into intracranial and orbital areas and compression of the upper maxillary sinus. Transthoracic echocardiogram reveals a 16 x 20 mm cyst in the left ventricle myocardium, with normal left ventricular systolic function (ejection fraction 66%). Chest CT shows a 33.05 x 12.27 mm low-density mass in the left ventricle. Upper abdominal CT identifies two round, low-density cystic lesions in liver segment seven, the largest being 28.09 x 27.32 mm, with multiple small vesicular structures (WHO type CE2). Abdominal CT reveals multiple unilocular cystic low-attenuation masses, the largest being type CE1 and measuring 60.44 x 54.40 mm, and a multiseptated mass of type CE2 in the pancreatic tail, measuring 44.96 x 51.43 mm. Pelvic CT shows an uneven distribution of intracystic density with WHO type CE3B daughter cysts, measuring 92.02 x 37.21 mm.
A: The primary diagnosis is disseminated cystic echinococcosis with involvement of the infratemporal fossa, liver, left ventricle, pancreas, and pelvis. Differential diagnoses could include other forms of cystic masses or tumors, but imaging and history strongly suggest echinococcosis. The prognosis depends on the response to treatment and potential complications from cyst rupture or organ dysfunction.
P: The management plan includes consultation with a multidisciplinary team to discuss treatment options, which may involve antiparasitic therapy, surgical intervention, or other targeted treatments depending on the cyst location and systemic involvement. A follow-up appointment will be scheduled to review treatment recommendations. The patient is advised to monitor symptoms and report any changes. Education on the nature of the disease and potential complications will be provided to ensure understanding and compliance with the treatment plan.

raw_note:
Doctor: Hello. I understand you've been admitted to the Infectious Disease Clinic due to suspected HFRS and dehydration. Can you tell me about your symptoms and when they started?
Patient: Hi, doc. About two weeks ago, I was cleaning out a cabin and got exposed to bank voles. Six days after that, I started feeling ill with fever, chills, weakness, and had difficulties eating and drinking. My urine production also decreased.
Doctor: I see. We ran some tests and found positive serology for Puumala virus, which confirms the HFRS diagnosis. Your laboratory tests taken the day before admission revealed thrombocytopenia with a platelet count of 48 × 10^9/L, impaired renal function with a creatinine level of 278 μmol/L, and leucocytosis with a white blood cell count of 14 × 10^9/L.
Patient: Oh, that sounds serious.
Doctor: Upon admission to the hospital, your platelet count had increased to 61 × 10^9/L and creatinine increased to 370 μmol/L, indicating clinical progression to the oliguric stage of HFRS. However, your platelet levels decreased to 12 × 10^9/L on days 8 to 9. Due to the high risk of spontaneous bleeding, we decided to transfuse platelets on days 8, 9, and 10.
Patient: Yes, I remember those transfusions.
Doctor: Despite the transfusions with three platelet units, you remained severely thrombocytopenic with platelet counts below 50 × 10^9/L during days 8 to 13. You also fulfilled the criteria for disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) from day 8.
Patient: That's concerning.
Doctor: On day 13, after your last platelet transfusion and a platelet count of 27 × 10^9/L, you experienced abdominal pain that increased in severity during the evening. We performed an abdominal computed tomography (CT) which showed congestion and ischemia in the terminal ileum due to a thrombus in the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) reaching up to the portal vein (PV).
Patient: Oh no, what does that mean?
Doctor: The hematologist advised against thrombolysis, which is a treatment to dissolve blood clots, due to your thrombocytopenia in combination with a known mild VHF. We will continue to monitor your condition closely and provide the necessary treatments to manage your symptoms and prevent complications.
Patient: I see. Thank you for explaining everything to me. What should I do now?
Doctor: For now, it's important that you stay hydrated, follow the prescribed medication regimen, and report any new or worsening symptoms to your healthcare team. We will continue to closely monitor your condition and adjust your treatment plan as needed.
Patient: Okay, thank you, doctor.
soap:
S: The patient, recently admitted to the Infectious Disease Clinic, reports symptoms starting approximately two weeks ago after exposure to bank voles, including fever, chills, weakness, decreased appetite, and reduced urine output. Symptoms began six days post-exposure.
O: Initial laboratory tests revealed thrombocytopenia (platelet count 48 × 10^9/L), impaired renal function (creatinine 278 μmol/L), and leucocytosis (white blood cell count 14 × 10^9/L). Subsequent tests showed worsening thrombocytopenia and renal function (platelet count 61 × 10^9/L, creatinine 370 μmol/L). Platelet transfusions were administered on days 8, 9, and 10 due to severe thrombocytopenia and criteria for disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) were met. Abdominal CT on day 13 revealed congestion and ischemia in the terminal ileum with a thrombus extending from the superior mesenteric vein to the portal vein.
A: The primary diagnosis is Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS) due to Puumala virus infection, confirmed by positive serology. Complications include severe thrombocytopenia, DIC, and thrombosis in the superior mesenteric and portal veins. Differential diagnosis includes other viral hemorrhagic fevers, but these are less likely given the serology results and exposure history.
P: Continue close monitoring of the patient's hematologic and renal parameters. Maintain hydration and adhere to the prescribed medication regimen. Avoid thrombolysis due to the risk of bleeding from severe thrombocytopenia. Regular follow-ups with the healthcare team are essential to adjust treatment plans based on the patient's evolving condition. Educate the patient on the importance of reporting new or worsening symptoms immediately.

base:
**SOAP Summary:**

- **Subjective:**
  - Patient reports fever, chills, weakness, and difficulty eating/drinking for 2 weeks.
  - Symptoms worsened over the past week, including decreased urine output.
  - Patient has a history of exposure to bank voles and recent positive serology for Puumala virus.
  - Patient has thrombocytopenia, impaired renal function, and leukocytosis.

- **Objective:**
  - Admission lab results: Platelet count 48 × 10^9/L, Creatinine 278 μmol/L.
  - Platelet count increased to 61 × 10^9/L and then decreased to 12 × 10^9/L.
  - Platelet transfusions given on days 8, 9, and 10.
  - Platelet count 27 × 10^9/L on day 13.
  - Abdominal CT scan shows thrombus in SMV reaching PV, leading to terminal ileum congestion/ischemia.

- **Assessment:**
  - Confirmed HFRS (Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome) by serology.
  - Oliguric stage of HFRS with thrombocytopenia and DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation).
  - Thrombocytopenia despite platelet transfusions.
  - Potential for spontaneous bleeding due to thrombocytopenia and VHF (Viral Hemorrhagic Fever).

- **Plan:**
  - Continue monitoring platelet count and renal function.
  - Provide hydration and necessary medications.
  - Monitor for signs of bleeding.
  - Consider further imaging if thrombus size increases.
  - Prepare for potential need for thrombolysis if thrombocytopenia improves but patient remains at high risk for bleeding.
  - Follow-up with hematologist and nephrologist as needed.

raw_note:
Doctor: Hello, I understand you presented to the ED with a 24-hour history of nausea, diarrhoea, lack of appetite, and abdominal pain. Can you tell me more about your symptoms?
Patient: Yeah, I've been feeling really sick for the past day. I've had nausea, diarrhoea, and I just can't eat anything. The pain in my stomach is mostly on the right side.
Doctor: Thank you for that information. We detected SARS-CoV-2 from the PCR of your NP swab. When you came to the ED, we noted pain and tenderness on the right side of your abdomen during the examination.
Patient: Oh, I see.
Doctor: We performed an abdominal ultrasound, and the findings were consistent with acute complicated appendicitis. To treat this, we started you on empiric IV antimicrobial treatment with cefotaxime and metronidazole.
Patient: Okay, so that's to help with the appendicitis?
Doctor: Yes, exactly. We also took you to the operating room for a laparoscopic appendectomy to remove your inflamed appendix.
Patient: Got it. And how did that go?
Doctor: The surgery went well. We took an abdominal fluid culture during the procedure, which revealed E. coli. We admitted you to the hospital within the first 24 hours from the onset of your symptoms.
Patient: I see. So, what now?
Doctor: During the surgery, we found intraoperative findings of peritonitis and broad intra-abdominal inflammation. This may indicate that your acute COVID-19 infection could have sped up the disease course of your acute appendicitis.
Patient: Oh, I didn't realize that could happen.
Doctor: Yes, it's not very common, but it can occur. We will continue to monitor your recovery and adjust your treatment accordingly. It's important to follow up with us and let us know if you experience any new or worsening symptoms.
Patient: Alright, thank you, doctor. I'll make sure to do that.
Doctor: You're welcome. We're here to help you through your recovery. Take care, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any concerns.
soap:
S: The patient, a 24-year-old male, presented with a 24-hour history of nausea, diarrhea, lack of appetite, and right-sided abdominal pain. He reported feeling "really sick" and unable to eat.
O: On examination in the ED, the patient exhibited right-sided abdominal pain and tenderness. A PCR test of an NP swab was positive for SARS-CoV-2. An abdominal ultrasound confirmed acute complicated appendicitis. During laparoscopic appendectomy, intraoperative findings included peritonitis and broad intra-abdominal inflammation. Abdominal fluid culture grew E. coli.
A: The primary diagnosis is acute complicated appendicitis, exacerbated by a concurrent acute COVID-19 infection. The presence of peritonitis and intra-abdominal inflammation suggests a severe infection, potentially accelerated by COVID-19.
P: The patient was started on empiric IV antimicrobial treatment with cefotaxime and metronidazole and underwent a successful laparoscopic appendectomy. Post-operative management includes continued monitoring of recovery and adjustment of treatment based on clinical progress. The patient is advised to follow up regularly and report any new or worsening symptoms. Further consultations with infectious disease specialists may be necessary to manage the COVID-19 aspect and its interaction with the appendicitis.

base:
**SOAP Summary:**

**Vital Signs:** Not provided

**History:**
- **Chief Complaint:** Nausea, diarrhea, loss of appetite, and abdominal pain for 24 hours.
- **Past Medical History:** Appendicitis, SARS-CoV-2 infection (PCR positive), no significant past medical history.

**Social History:**
- **Smoking:** No
- **Alcohol:** No
- **Substance Abuse:** No
- **Medications:** None at presentation
- **Activities:** Worked as a construction worker

**Review of Systems:**
- **Gastrointestinal:** Nausea, diarrhea, loss of appetite, and abdominal pain (right-sided)
- **Abdomen:** Pain and tenderness on the right side of the abdomen
- **General:** Fever, chills, fatigue

**Physical Examination:**
- **General:** Alert and oriented, slightly pale, diaphoretic
- **Head and Neck:** No abnormalities
- **Chest:** No rales, no wheezes, clear breath sounds bilaterally
- **Heart:** Regular rhythm, no murmurs, no rubs
- **Abdomen:** Tenderness on palpation, guarding, and rebound tenderness, positive Murphy’s sign
- **Extremities:** No edema, no clubbing, no cyanosis

**Diagnostic Studies:**
- **Laboratory:** Negative CBC, negative CRP, negative ESR, negative stool WBC, negative urine WBC, negative blood cultures, negative blood glucose
- **Imaging:** Abdominal ultrasound showing acute complicated appendicitis, peritonitis, and broad intra-abdominal inflammation
- **Culture:** Abdominal fluid culture showing E. coli

**Procedures:**
- **Surgical:** Laparoscopic appendectomy, intraoperative peritonitis and broad intra-abdominal inflammation confirmed

**Plan:**
- **Antimicrobial Therapy:** Empiric IV cefotaxime and metronidazole
- **Hospital Admission:** Within 24 hours of symptom onset
- **Follow-Up:** Monitoring for complications, including intra-abdominal inflammation, and adjusting treatment as necessary
- **Infection Control:** Consideration of COVID-19-related complications
- **Education:** Patient to follow up with healthcare provider and report any new or worsening symptoms

Doctor: Hello, I see that you've come to the National Institute of Integrative Medicine Clinic in Melbourne in June 2016. How can I help you today?
Patient: Hi, my daughter has been experiencing chronic pain, extreme muscle wasting which requires a wheelchair, growth retardation, and she's severely underweight at 20 kg. She also has swollen and painful joints, heart palpitations, loose stools, and headaches.
Doctor: I'm sorry to hear that. It seems like her condition is causing extreme weakness, not allowing her to move her limbs without assistance. So, she's not able to feed herself, move her legs, or even stand up, right?
Patient: Yes, that's correct. She's been so ill that she hasn't been able to attend school for several months now, and she's been in and out of the hospital regularly.
Doctor: I see that she was diagnosed with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) three years ago and has been treated with standard medications for the potential autoimmune condition. This includes regular corticosteroid infusions with methylprednisolone, treatment with methotrexate, and anakinra, which is a recombinant and modified interleukin-one-receptor-antagonist.
Patient: Yes, but despite these treatments, her condition has worsened over the past three years. By the time we came here, her prognosis was extremely critical.
Doctor: I understand. It looks like her illness started suddenly three years ago with a high fever and rashes. This coincided with the illness and sudden death of her pet dog, which had been experiencing a wobbly walk, weight loss, and listlessness before its accidental death with an open wound and blood loss. Is that correct?
Patient: Yes, that's right. She was exposed to the dog's blood in her eyes when it passed away. We're worried it might be a possible infection from the dog.
Doctor: That's a plausible path of infection. Our research lab here at NIIM has developed a two-part Pathogen Blood Test assay that combines cytological microscopy and genetic analysis of the pathogen by polymerase-chain-reaction (PCR) DNA analysis. We can use this test to help identify any possible infection in your daughter.
Patient: Oh, that sounds promising. What do we need to do for this test?
Doctor: We'll need to take a fresh blood sample from your daughter and process it for the test. The results will help us determine if there is an infection and guide us towards the best treatment options.
Patient: Okay, thank you. We're hopeful this test will provide some answers and help improve her condition.
Doctor: You're welcome. Let's proceed with the blood test, and we'll discuss the results and next steps once we have them. We're here to help your daughter get better.

Doctor: Hello, I see that you've presented with progressive, PG-like lesions on your left arm. Can you tell me how this happened?
Patient: Yeah, I had a minor abrasive trauma in a playground accident. It's been getting worse since then.
Doctor: I see. According to your medical history, you were diagnosed with LAD-1 at 18 months of age. Your CD18 expression level was 5-10%, which indicates a mild/moderate phenotype. Have you ever experienced any life-threatening wounds before?
Patient: No, never.
Doctor: Your most recent clinical course involved 3.5 weeks of IV antibiotics and multiple wound debridement procedures at a peripheral site. The lesions significantly expanded after debridement, consistent with PG-like wounds. You were then transferred to our tertiary center for better care.
Patient: Yes, that's right.
Doctor: Our team here includes experts from pediatrics for general medical care and care coordination, immunology for directing immunosuppressive therapies, infectious disease for guiding culture-directed antibiotic and antifungal therapies, and plastic surgery for overseeing wound care, debridement, and acquisition of biopsies.
Patient: That sounds comprehensive.
Doctor: We performed a punch biopsy on the central wound, and the histology demonstrated necrosis, focal superficial ulceration of the epidermis, and mixed inflammatory infiltrate including histiocytes, multinucleated giant cells, lymphocytes, and focal neutrophils. Interestingly, there was a lack of dermal neutrophilia, which is consistent with PG-like disease.
Patient: Hmm, I see.
Doctor: To treat this, we've initiated systemic therapy consisting of prednisone (1 mg/kg/d), cyclosporine (goal trough level 100-200 ng/mL), and intravenous immunoglobulin (1 g/kg every 2 weeks). We also treated the wounds empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics, including meropenem (500 mg IV q8h) and vancomycin (500 mg IV q12h).
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: On day 104, a tissue culture obtained during debridement showed a deep infection with Fusarium, an invasive fungal infection typically occurring in immunocompromised patients. This corresponded to clinical worsening and elevation in your C-reactive protein levels.
Patient: Oh no, that's not good.
Doctor: We initially treated the infection with dual liposomal amphotericin (10 mg/kg/d IV) and voriconazole (9 mg/kg/dose IV q12). However, we have now stepped down to liposomal amphotericin alone. We'll continue to monitor your progress closely.
Patient: I appreciate the care you're providing. Thank you, doctor.
Doctor: You're welcome. We'll do our best to help you recover. If you have any concerns or questions, please don't hesitate to reach out.

S: The patient, with a history of LAD-1 diagnosed at 18 months and mild/moderate phenotype (CD18 expression 5-10%), presented with progressive, pyoderma gangrenosum (PG)-like lesions on the left arm following minor abrasive trauma in a playground accident. The patient denies any history of life-threatening wounds.
O: The patient underwent multiple wound debridements and 3.5 weeks of IV antibiotics at a peripheral site, after which the lesions expanded, consistent with PG-like wounds. Histology from a punch biopsy showed necrosis, superficial ulceration, and mixed inflammatory infiltrate but lacked dermal neutrophilia. Recent tissue culture revealed a deep Fusarium infection. Current medications include prednisone (1 mg/kg/d), cyclosporine (goal trough level 100-200 ng/mL), intravenous immunoglobulin (1 g/kg every 2 weeks), meropenem (500 mg IV q8h), vancomycin (500 mg IV q12h), and liposomal amphotericin (10 mg/kg/d IV). C-reactive protein levels are elevated.
A: The primary diagnosis is PG-like lesions secondary to LAD-1 with a complication of deep Fusarium infection. Differential diagnosis includes other causes of necrotic ulcers but is less likely given the patient's specific history and biopsy results.
P: Continue current systemic therapy and antibiotic regimen. Step down antifungal treatment to liposomal amphotericin alone due to the Fusarium infection. Regular monitoring of infection status and adjustment of medications as needed. Coordination of care with pediatrics, immunology, infectious disease, and plastic surgery for comprehensive management. Educate the patient on signs of infection worsening and ensure adherence to the treatment regimen.

**Vital Signs:** Stable

**Subjective Data:**
- Patient reports a gradual onset of progressive, PG-like lesions on their left arm following a minor abrasion from a playground accident.
- Patient has a history of LAD-1 (Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency Type 1) diagnosed at 18 months with a CD18 expression level of 5-10%.
- Previous life-threatening wounds have not occurred.
- Patient underwent 3.5 weeks of IV antibiotics and multiple wound debridement procedures at a peripheral site, followed by transfer to a tertiary center for further care.

- Histopathology lacked dermal neutrophilia, indicative of PG-like disease.
- Tissue culture from debridement showed deep infection with Fusarium, an invasive fungal infection typical in immunocompromised patients.

**Assessment:**
- PG-like Lesions (consistent with LAD-1)
- Invasive Fungal Infection (Fusarium)

**Plan:**
- Systemic Therapy:
  - Prednisone (1 mg/kg/d)
  - Cyclosporine (goal trough level 100-200 ng/mL)
  - Intravenous Immunoglobulin (1 g/kg every 2 weeks)
- Wound Care:
  - Broad-spectrum antibiotics: Meropenem (500 mg IV q8h) and Vancomycin (500 mg IV q12h)
- Monitoring:
  - C-Reactive Protein Levels
  - Progress of Invasive Fungal Infection
  - Response to Treatment

Doctor: Hello, I see you've been admitted to our institution recently. Can you tell me what symptoms you've been experiencing?
Patient: Yeah, I've been having this left-sided testicular pain for about a month, and a dry cough. In the last four days, I've also had a fever, felt really weak and tired, and my appetite has gone down.
Doctor: I see. I also noticed from your history that you have insulin-requiring type 2 diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension, stage 3 chronic renal disease, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Is that correct?
Patient: Yes, that's right.
Doctor: During your physical examination, you were found to be febrile and tachycardic. We also noticed left testicular edema and pain on epididymal palpation. We conducted some laboratory tests and found neutrophilic leukocytosis with 24.2 x103/uL WBC and 87% neutrophils. Your chest X-ray showed thickening of the bronchial walls, but no concomitant parenchymal abnormalities. The urinalysis showed an increase in white blood cells and a few Gram-negative bacteria. Have you been informed about these results?
Patient: Yeah, the doctor mentioned something about that.
Doctor: We also performed a testicular ultrasound which revealed an abscess in the tail of your left epididymis. Because of this, we started you on oral doxycycline therapy. However, on the fourth day, you developed worsening respiratory symptoms and inadequate metabolic control with HbA1c at 8.7% and central glycemia at 510 mg/dL. This required your transfer to the intermediate-care unit for management of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state. 
Patient: I remember that, it was a tough time.
Doctor: We then conducted a CT of your abdomen and pelvis, which was normal. However, a follow-up chest X-ray showed multiple bilateral nodular lesions and left pleural effusion. Your blood and urine cultures came back positive for K. pneumoniae, which is sensitive to penicillin, quinolone, and carbapenem. 
Patient: Oh, I didn't know about that.
Doctor: A CT of your chest confirmed the presence of multiple bilateral nodules with halo sign and reversed halo sign. Given the presence of an extrapulmonary infective focus, your respiratory symptoms, the isolation of bacteria in your blood, and the multiple bilateral nodules in the chest CT scan, we considered the diagnosis of SPE secondary to epididymitis. As a result, we decided to suspend the doxycycline and start intravenous ciprofloxacin.
Patient: Okay, so what's the plan now?
Doctor: Your clinical evolution after a 14-day antibiotic course has been satisfactory. There's been an improvement in your respiratory symptoms and a marked decrease in your testicular pain and edema. Your chest X-ray also showed improvement. We will continue to monitor your progress and make any necessary adjustments to your treatment plan.
Patient: That's good to hear. Thank you, doctor.
soap:
S: The patient, a male with a history of insulin-requiring type 2 diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension, stage 3 chronic renal disease, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, reports experiencing left-sided testicular pain for about a month, accompanied by a dry cough. Over the past four days, he has developed a fever, general weakness, fatigue, and decreased appetite.
O: Physical examination revealed fever, tachycardia, left testicular edema, and pain on epididymal palpation. Laboratory tests showed neutrophilic leukocytosis (WBC 24.2 x103/uL with 87% neutrophils), urinalysis indicated increased white blood cells and presence of Gram-negative bacteria. Chest X-ray demonstrated bronchial wall thickening without parenchymal abnormalities. Testicular ultrasound revealed an abscess in the tail of the left epididymis. Follow-up imaging included a CT of the abdomen and pelvis (normal), and chest X-ray and CT showing multiple bilateral nodular lesions, left pleural effusion, and halo signs. Blood and urine cultures were positive for K. pneumoniae, sensitive to penicillin, quinolone, and carbapenem. The patient's HbA1c was 8.7% and central glycemia was 510 mg/dL.
A: The primary diagnosis is septic pulmonary embolism (SPE) secondary to epididymitis, complicated by hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state. Differential diagnoses included other sources of infection due to immunocompromised state from chronic conditions. The prognosis is cautiously optimistic given the current improvement with treatment.
P: The management plan includes continuation of intravenous ciprofloxacin with close monitoring of antibiotic efficacy and potential side effects. The patient's diabetes and other chronic conditions will be managed aggressively to prevent further complications. Regular follow-up imaging and labs will be conducted to monitor the resolution of pulmonary and testicular findings. Education on infection signs, glucose control, and compliance with the treatment regimen will be reinforced. Referrals to endocrinology for diabetes management and urology for ongoing testicular issues are planned.

Laboratory tests indicated neutrophilic leukocytosis (WBC 24.2 x10^3/uL, 87% neutrophils), increased white blood cells in urinalysis, and Gram-negative bacteria. Testicular ultrasound showed an abscess in the tail of the left epididymis. Chest X-ray showed thickening of the bronchial walls without parenchymal abnormalities. CT of the abdomen and pelvis was normal. Follow-up chest X-ray revealed multiple bilateral nodular lesions and left pleural effusion. Blood and urine cultures were positive for K. pneumoniae, sensitive to penicillin, quinolone, and carbapenem. CT of the chest confirmed multiple bilateral nodules with halo sign and reversed halo sign.
A: The primary diagnosis is secondary pulmonary syndrome (SPE) secondary to epididymitis, complicated by hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state and multiple bilateral nodular lesions suggestive of disseminated infection. Differential diagnoses include other causes of pulmonary nodules and systemic infections.
P: Initiated treatment with oral doxycycline, later switched to intravenous ciprofloxacin due to the presence of an extrapulmonary infective focus and improvement in clinical symptoms. Continued monitoring of the patient's response to antibiotics and adjustment of the treatment plan as needed. Education provided regarding the importance of adherence to the prescribed treatment regimen and monitoring of diabetes and other comorbid conditions.

**SOAP Summary:**

- **S (Subjective):**
  - Patient reports left-sided testicular pain for one month, accompanied by a dry cough, fever, weakness, and loss of appetite over the past four days.
  - History includes insulin-requiring type 2 diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension, stage 3 chronic renal disease, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
  - Fever, tachycardia noted during physical examination.
  - Left testicular edema and pain on epididymal palpation observed.
  - Laboratory findings: Neutrophilic leukocytosis (WBC 24.2 x10^3/uL, 87% neutrophils), increased white blood cells, and Gram-negative bacteria in urinalysis.
  - Chest X-ray shows thickened bronchial walls without parenchymal abnormalities.
  - Testicular ultrasound reveals an abscess in the tail of the left epididymis.
  - Transfer to intermediate-care unit due to worsening respiratory symptoms and inadequate metabolic control (HbA1c 8.7%, central glycemia 510 mg/dL).
  - CT of abdomen and pelvis normal; follow-up chest X-ray shows multiple bilateral nodular lesions and left pleural effusion.
  - Blood and urine cultures positive for K. pneumoniae, sensitive to penicillin, quinolone, and carbapenem.
  - CT of chest confirms multiple bilateral nodules with halo sign and reversed halo sign.

- **O (Objective):**
  - Physical examination findings: Fever, tachycardia, left testicular edema and pain on epididymal palpation.
  - Laboratory findings: Elevated WBC count, neutrophilia, increased white blood cells, and Gram-negative bacteria in urinalysis.
  - Imaging studies: Normal CT of abdomen and pelvis, follow-up chest X-ray showing multiple bilateral nodular lesions and left pleural effusion, CT of chest confirming multiple bilateral nodules with halo sign and reversed halo sign.

- **P (Plan):**
  - Initiated oral doxycycline therapy for suspected epididymitis.
  - Transferred to intermediate-care unit for management of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state.
  - Switched to intravenous ciprofloxacin due to positive blood culture and multiple bilateral nodules in chest CT scan.
  - Continued monitoring and adjustment of treatment plan as needed.

Doctor: Hello, how can I help you today?
Patient: Hi, I've been having some swelling below my tongue and pain during meals.
Doctor: I see. Let's do an examination to find out what's causing the issue. 
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: *After examining* I can feel a hard swelling on the left submandibular gland duct. We will need to take an occlusal radiograph to confirm what's causing the swelling.
Patient: Sure, let's do that.
Doctor: *After taking radiograph* The radiograph confirms that there is radio-opacity medial to teeth 46 and 47. I have diagnosed the swelling as left submandibular salivary gland duct sialolithiasis, which means you have a stone in your salivary gland duct.
Patient: Oh, I see. So what's the next step?
Doctor: We will perform a minor procedure to remove the stone while you are under local anesthesia. We will place a stay suture to prevent the stone from gliding posteriorly, then make an incision over the stone to expose the calculi and facilitate its removal.
Patient: Alright, if that's what it takes.
Doctor: *After performing the procedure* The stone has been successfully removed. It was roughly oval and measured 10.4 mm. We sutured the duct at the level of the mucosa on the floor of the mouth.
Patient: That's a relief. Thank you.
Doctor: You're welcome. We will need to schedule follow-up evaluations to ensure there are no postoperative complications.
Patient: Sure, just let me know when I should come in.
Doctor: I'll have my assistant schedule an appointment for you. In the meantime, if you experience any discomfort or notice any issues, don't hesitate to contact our office.
Patient: Thank you, Doctor. I appreciate your help.
Doctor: You're welcome. Take care and see you at your follow-up appointment.

P: Performed a minor surgical procedure to remove the salivary stone. Postoperative care includes follow-up evaluations to monitor for complications. Patient instructed to contact the office if experiencing discomfort or other issues. Follow-up appointment to be scheduled by the assistant.

Doctor: Hello, I see that you're a 77-year-old male patient with a history of myocardial infarction for half a year. Have you experienced any significant discomfort during this time?
Patient: No, not really. I've been living a normal life at home without any major issues.
Doctor: Alright. Can you tell me what happened two hours before you were admitted to the hospital?
Patient: I suddenly developed significant palpitations and shortness of breath. It was really scary.
Doctor: I see. You were then admitted to the Second Hospital of Lanzhou University by ambulance. When we examined you, your expression was painful, and we heard a distinct murmur in the precordium. We also performed an Electrocardiogram, which showed old left ventricular anteroseptal myocardial infarction. An X-ray confirmed an enlarged heart shadow. However, your myocardial enzymes and other laboratory test results showed no significant abnormalities. 
Patient: Oh, I see.
Doctor: Echocardiography revealed that the interventricular septum was thinned near the apex and bulged toward the right ventricular side, with a rupture opening of about 8 mm in diameter visible on its top (Fig.). CDFI showed that left ventricular blood shunted to the right ventricle through the rupture (Fig.), with a shunt velocity measured by CW of about 410 cm/s, and a PG of 67 mmHg.
Patient: Hmm, that sounds serious.
Doctor: Yes, it is. The echocardiography also showed left ventricular enlargement with an anteroposterior diameter of about 61 mm, significantly reduced wall motion amplitude, a left ventricular ejection fraction (EF) of about 33%, moderate aortic and mitral regurgitation, and pulmonary artery systolic pressure of about 60 mmHg. The echocardiographic diagnosis is old myocardial infarction of ventricular septal apex with ventricular septal rupture.
Patient: So, what can be done about it, doc?
Doctor: Due to your older age and reduced left ventricular function, surgical repair of the site of ventricular septal rupture would be very difficult. After multidisciplinary discussion, it was agreed that you couldn't undergo thoracotomy and were not suitable for thoracotomy. Instead, echocardiography guided interventional occlusion of the ruptured interventricular septum could be performed.
Patient: I see. So, what's next?
Doctor: We will need your family's consent for the interventional occlusion procedure. Once they have signed a written consent, we can proceed with the treatment.
Patient: Okay, I'll talk to my family about it.
(After obtaining the family's consent)
Doctor: Great, we've received the consent. Under the guidance of transesophageal echocardiography, we will clearly display the ventricular septal rupture during the surgery. We will measure the size of the rupture again to ensure the proper occluder device is used to prevent it from falling off.
Patient: I understand. Thank you, doctor. I hope the procedure goes well.
Doctor: You're welcome. We'll do our best to provide you with the best possible care and treatment.
soap:
S: The 77-year-old male patient with a history of myocardial infarction reported sudden onset of palpitations and shortness of breath two hours before admission. He described the episode as scary but noted no significant discomfort in the months following his myocardial infarction until this event.
O: Upon examination, the patient appeared in pain with a distinct murmur in the precordium. Electrocardiogram confirmed an old left ventricular anteroseptal myocardial infarction. Chest X-ray showed an enlarged heart shadow. Myocardial enzymes and other lab tests were normal. Echocardiography revealed a thinned interventricular septum near the apex bulging toward the right ventricular side with an 8 mm rupture. Left ventricular blood was noted to shunt to the right ventricle, with a shunt velocity of 410 cm/s and a pressure gradient of 67 mmHg. Additional findings included left ventricular enlargement, reduced wall motion amplitude, an ejection fraction of 33%, moderate aortic and mitral regurgitation, and pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 60 mmHg.
A: The primary diagnosis is an old myocardial infarction with ventricular septal rupture. The patient's age and reduced left ventricular function make surgical repair challenging. Differential diagnoses were considered but ruled out based on imaging and lab results.
P: The management plan includes echocardiography-guided interventional occlusion of the ruptured interventricular septum, as the patient is not a candidate for thoracotomy. The procedure will proceed following family consent, which has been obtained. The size of the rupture will be measured again during surgery to ensure the correct occluder device is used. The patient and family will be kept informed throughout the process to ensure understanding and compliance with the treatment plan.

base:
### SOAP Summary

**Subjective:**
- Patient is a 77-year-old male with a history of myocardial infarction for half a year.
- Recently, he experienced significant palpitations and shortness of breath, which were sudden and frightening.
- On admission, he had a painful expression and a murmur in the precordium.
- ECG showed old left ventricular anteroseptal myocardial infarction.
- X-ray confirmed an enlarged heart shadow.
- Laboratory tests were normal, but echocardiography revealed a ventricular septal rupture with a diameter of approximately 8 mm.

**Objective:**
- Echocardiography showed:
  - Thinning of the interventricular septum near the apex and bulging toward the right ventricular side.
  - A 8 mm diameter rupture opening.
  - Left ventricular blood shunting to the right ventricle through the rupture.
  - Shunt velocity of about 410 cm/s and pulmonary artery systolic pressure of about 67 mmHg.
  - Left ventricular enlargement with an anteroposterior diameter of about 61 mm.
  - Reduced wall motion amplitude and left ventricular ejection fraction (EF) of about 33%.
  - Moderate aortic and mitral regurgitation.

**Assessment:**
- Patient has a history of myocardial infarction and now presents with a ventricular septal rupture.
- Given the patient’s advanced age and reduced left ventricular function, surgical repair is challenging.
- Multidisciplinary consensus suggests that interventional occlusion of the ventricular septal rupture should be performed under echocardiographic guidance.

**Plan:**
- Obtain family consent for the interventional occlusion procedure.
- Use transesophageal echocardiography to guide the procedure.
- Ensure proper sizing of the occluder device to prevent it from falling off.

raw_note:
Doctor: Hello, based on your past medical history, I see that you have DMD, scoliosis and have had multiple back surgeries, failure to thrive, and atrial fibrillation. How can I help you today?
Patient: Hi, Doctor. I've had abdominal pain and vomiting for the past two days.
Doctor: I see. Have you had any issues with constipation?
Patient: Yeah, I've been chronically constipated since I was 15 years old. I only have one or two bowel movements per month and I don't use laxatives.
Doctor: Okay, let's do a quick check-up. On admission, I noticed that you're afebrile, tachycardic with a heart rate of 148 beats per minute, hypotensive with a blood pressure of 89/55 mmHg, and tachypneic with a respiratory rate of 36/min. You also appear cachectic with severe muscle wasting and dry mucous membranes. Your body mass index (BMI) is 14.8 kg/m2. 
Patient: Oh, that doesn't sound good.
Doctor: Let's continue with the physical exam. I noticed that your abdomen is soft, non-tender, and distended, with no guarding and rigidity. You also have hypoactive bowel sounds and chronic muscle contractions in your extremities. 
Patient: Hmm, yes, I feel that too.
Doctor: We also ran some laboratory tests. Your leukocytosis is 20300/µL with a left shift (bands 22), which is quite elevated. Your hemoglobin is 16.4 g/dL and your hematocrit is 51.1%, both within the normal range. Your blood urea nitrogen (BUN) is 31 mg/dL, which is elevated, while your creatinine is 0.40 mg/dL, which is low. There's a mild elevation in your aspartate aminotransferase (AST) at 54 IU/L. Your prothrombin time (PT) is 14s, international normalized ratio (INR) is 1.3, and partial thromboplastin time (PTT) is 32.3. Lastly, your albumin level is normal at 4.7 g/dL.
Patient: So, what does all that mean?
Doctor: It means that you're severely intravascular volume-depleted, as indicated by your physical exam and laboratory values. We'll need to start you on aggressive fluid resuscitation with Ringer's lactate to address this issue.
Patient: Okay, thank you for explaining everything, Doctor. What should I do next?
Doctor: You should continue to monitor your symptoms and follow up with your primary care physician. If your abdominal pain or vomiting worsens or if you develop any new concerning symptoms, please come back to the hospital immediately.
Patient: Got it. Thank you so much for your help, Doctor. I'll follow your advice.
Doctor: You're welcome. Take care and feel better soon!
soap:
S: The patient, a known case of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), scoliosis, multiple back surgeries, failure to thrive, and atrial fibrillation, presents with abdominal pain and vomiting for the past two days. He reports chronic constipation since age 15, with only one or two bowel movements per month, and does not use laxatives.
O: On examination, the patient is afebrile, tachycardic with a heart rate of 148 bpm, hypotensive with a blood pressure of 89/55 mmHg, and tachypneic with a respiratory rate of 36/min. He appears cachectic with severe muscle wasting and dry mucous membranes, and his BMI is 14.8 kg/m2. Abdominal examination reveals a soft, non-tender, and distended abdomen with hypoactive bowel sounds and no guarding or rigidity. Laboratory tests show leukocytosis at 20300/µL with a left shift, hemoglobin 16.4 g/dL, hematocrit 51.1%, BUN 31 mg/dL, creatinine 0.40 mg/dL, AST 54 IU/L, PT 14s, INR 1.3, PTT 32.3s, and normal albumin at 4.7 g/dL.
A: The patient is diagnosed with severe intravascular volume depletion likely secondary to dehydration and chronic constipation. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal obstruction and underlying infection due to leukocytosis.
P: Initiate aggressive fluid resuscitation with Ringer's lactate. Monitor vital signs and laboratory values closely. Advise the patient to monitor symptoms and follow up with his primary care physician. Instruct the patient to return to the hospital if abdominal pain, vomiting worsens, or new symptoms develop. Consider consultation with gastroenterology for further evaluation of chronic constipation and potential gastrointestinal obstruction.

Patient is fully vaccinated against COVID-19 with Pfizer vaccine, last dose received in February 2021.
O: Vital signs include blood pressure 120/71 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 85% on room air. Patient is afebrile. Laboratory tests confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. Initial chest X-ray showed mildly patchy bibasilar pulmonary infiltrates and a calcified pulmonary nodule in the mid-right lung measuring 2.0 cm. Repeat chest X-ray indicated slight interval improvement of bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Current oxygen requirement is 4-5 L/min via nasal cannula.
A: Primary diagnosis of COVID-19 with respiratory involvement, complicated by underlying chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Differential diagnosis includes exacerbation of COPD. The presence of a calcified pulmonary nodule suggests a potential chronic pathology, possibly benign given the calcification, but requires further evaluation.
P: Continue supplemental oxygen at 4-5 L/min via nasal cannula and current medications including remdesivir, dexamethasone, furosemide, azithromycin, and enoxaparin. Monitor clinical and oxygenation status closely with adjustments in therapy as needed. Consider further imaging or biopsy of the pulmonary nodule if there is no improvement or if new symptoms suggest malignancy. Educate patient on COVID-19 management and the importance of monitoring symptoms. Discuss potential need for prolonged hospital stay depending on respiratory status and response to treatment.

**SOAP Summary:**

**Subjective:**
- Patient reports shortness of breath, cough, myalgias, and malaise for about a week.
- She has a past medical history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and long-term tobacco abuse.
- Recently quit smoking.
- Has received the Pfizer COVID vaccine with her second dose in February 2021.
- Positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR test result.

**Objective:**
- Vital Signs: Blood Pressure - 120/71 mmHg, Heart Rate - 78 bpm, Respiratory Rate - 20 breaths/min, Oxygen Saturation - 85% on room air, Afebrile.
- Chest X-ray shows:
  - Mildly patchy bibasilar pulmonary infiltrates.
  - Calcified pulmonary nodule in the mid-right lung measuring 2.0 cm.
- Repeat Chest X-ray after admission shows slight interval improvement of bilateral pulmonary infiltrates.

**Assessment:**
- Acute exacerbation of COPD with recent SARS-CoV-2 infection.
- Possible post-viral syndrome or superimposed pneumonia.

**Plan:**
- Oxygen therapy: 4-5 L per minute via nasal cannula.
- Medications: Remdesivir, Dexamethasone, Furosemide, Azithromycin, Enoxaparin for VTE prophylaxis.
- Close monitoring and adjustment of treatment as necessary.
- Potential for prolonged hospital stay for recovery and symptom management.

Doctor: Hello, I have reviewed your medical history, and it seems that you've been experiencing mild low back pain for the past 3 years. Can you tell me more about your symptoms?
Patient: Yeah, that's correct. But in the last 20 days, the pain has worsened severely, and it's now radiating to the back of my lower limbs. I've also had a lot of difficulty walking.
Doctor: I see. When I examined your lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints, I found that the palpation was painful, which is a positive Patrick's test. We also did some laboratory analysis, and your inflammatory markers, such as reactive C protein, were found to be normal. Are you aware of any other health issues or habits that could be related to your condition?
Patient: Well, I am a heavy smoker, but I don't have any other comorbidities.
Doctor: Thank you for that information. We performed an MRI, which demonstrated an infiltrative mass affecting the vertebral body and pedicles of your L4 vertebra. There was also heterogeneous enhancement after the contrast administration. The tumor has extended into the distal third of your inferior vena cava and the left iliopsoas muscle.
Patient: Oh, that doesn't sound good.
Doctor: We also performed a CT scan, which showed a lytic lesion with indistinct borders in the vertebral body of your fourth lumbar vertebra (L4). There was a soft tissue component that dislocated the aorta and the inferior vena cava. Additionally, there was a typical "ring-and-arc" chondroid matrix mineralization in the L4 vertebral body.
Patient: What does this all mean, doc?
Doctor: We decided to perform a needle transpedicular biopsy of the vertebral lesion to better understand your condition. The biopsy revealed a malignant infiltrative bone neoplasm with chondroid differentiation and several areas of necrosis.
Patient: So, what's the next step?
Doctor: Three weeks after the needle biopsy, we performed a posterior lumbar approach, installing pedicle screws at L3 and L5, and removed the posterior elements. We also dissected the anterior large vessels from the spine and performed an "en bloc" resection of L4. We reconstructed your lumbar spine with a titanium cage and an anterolateral plate.
Patient: And what were the results of that?
Doctor: The histopathological examination of the resected L4 vertebral body revealed an infiltrative malignant biphasic neoplasia composed of ample ill-defined areas.
Patient: What does this mean for my future treatment?
Doctor: We will need to discuss further treatment options, including potential chemotherapy and radiation therapy, to target and manage the malignant neoplasm. It is crucial that you follow up with your medical team regularly to monitor your progress and adjust treatment as necessary.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I appreciate your help and guidance throughout this process.

O: Physical examination revealed pain on palpation of the lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints, with a positive Patrick's test. Laboratory analysis showed normal inflammatory markers, including reactive C protein. MRI and CT scans identified an infiltrative mass in the L4 vertebral body, extending into the inferior vena cava and left iliopsoas muscle, with lytic lesions and "ring-and-arc" chondroid matrix mineralization. A needle transpedicular biopsy indicated a malignant infiltrative bone neoplasm with chondroid differentiation. Post-surgical histopathology confirmed infiltrative malignant biphasic neoplasia.
A: The primary diagnosis is a malignant infiltrative bone neoplasm with chondroid differentiation at the L4 vertebra. Differential diagnoses could include other types of primary spinal tumors or metastatic disease. The prognosis depends on the response to further oncological treatments and the extent of disease spread.
P: The patient underwent surgical resection of the L4 vertebra and spinal reconstruction. The next steps include discussing additional treatments such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy. Regular follow-ups with the oncology team are essential to monitor the patient's progress and adjust the treatment plan as necessary. Smoking cessation should be strongly advised to support overall health and treatment efficacy.

**SOAP Summary:**

**Vital Signs:** Not provided in the given note.

**Subjective Data:**
- **Pain:** Mild low back pain for 3 years, exacerbated over the past 20 days, now radiating to the back of lower limbs, accompanied by difficulty in walking.
- **History of Present Illness:** Patient reports worsening pain over the past month, with recent onset of radicular pain and difficulty in ambulation.
- **Past Medical History:** Heavy smoker; no other significant comorbidities.
- **Social History:** No significant family history or risk factors noted.
- **Review of Systems:** No significant abnormalities reported.

**Objective Data:**
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Lumbar spine and sacroiliac joint tenderness on palpation (positive Patrick's test).
  - Normal inflammatory markers (CRP) in blood tests.
  - Positive findings on MRI and CT scans showing an infiltrative mass affecting the vertebral body and pedicles of L4 vertebra, extending into the inferior vena cava and left iliopsoas muscle.
  - Histopathological examination of the resected L4 vertebral body reveals an infiltrative malignant biphasic neoplasm composed of ill-defined areas.

**Assessment:**
- **Diagnosis:** Malignant infiltrative bone neoplasm with chondroid differentiation (likely Ewing’s sarcoma/ peripheral primitive neuroectodermal tumor [PNET]).
- **Prognosis:** Requires multidisciplinary management including chemotherapy and radiation therapy post-resection.
- **Plan:**
  - Needle transpedicular biopsy followed by posterior lumbar approach for en bloc resection of L4 vertebral body.
  - Postoperative chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
  - Regular follow-up with oncology team for monitoring and adjustment of treatment as needed.

Doctor: Hello, how can I help you today?
Patient: Hi, I was hospitalized a while back because of these painful skin ulcers I had, and I'm not sure what's going on with them.
Doctor: I see. Can you tell me more about the ulcers?
Patient: They're in the mammary region and have these red and purplish edges. They started about 6 months ago and have rapidly developed from pustular lesions.
Doctor: Oh, I understand. Have you tried any treatments?
Patient: Yes, I've tried antiseptic and antibiotic therapies, but they didn't help.
Doctor: Alright, let me check your medical records. It seems a skin biopsy was done, and it showed a dermal-hypodermal neutrophilic infiltrate, suggesting PG. The laboratory work-up ruled out any underlying inflammatory condition. You were given pulse-therapy with intravenous methylprednisolone 125 mg daily for 5 consecutive days, and there was clinical improvement. You were also prescribed prednisone at tapering dosages in combination with cyclosporine 300 mg daily, which led to progressive healing of the lesions. Is that correct?
Patient: Yes, that's right.
Doctor: During your hospitalization, a chest computerized tomography revealed a multinodular goiter. Neck ultrasound showed a 12.4 mm solid hypoechoic nodule in the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe and an 8.5 mm hypoechoic nodule in the lower pole of the same lobe. A fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the dominant nodule was performed, and the cytological exam resulted in Tir4 category, which is "suspicious for thyroid carcinoma." You underwent a total thyroidectomy, and the histological examination was consistent with multicentric classical PTC, with focal extension to extra-thyroidal soft tissues and surgical resection margins. The histopathologic stadium was pT3(m)Nx according to the American Joint Committee on Cancer TNM VII Edition Staging System. Is that all correct?
Patient: Yes, that's what happened.
Doctor: After the surgery, you received radioactive iodine ablation treatment with 3700 MBq under human recombinant α-thyrotropin stimulation (Thyrogen®). The post-treatment Whole Body Scan showed no uptake outside the thyroid bed, and the stimulated Tg serum value was 2.6 ng/ml. These findings suggest that the treatment has been effective.
Patient: Oh, that's good news!
Doctor: Yes, it is. Now, it's essential to monitor your progress and follow up with your healthcare team regularly. Make sure to report any new symptoms or concerns promptly.
Patient: Okay, I'll do that. Thank you, doctor.
Doctor: You're welcome. Take care, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns in the future.

S: The patient reports having painful skin ulcers in the mammary region with red and purplish edges, which started about 6 months ago and developed from pustular lesions. The patient has a history of hospitalization for these ulcers and has tried antiseptic and antibiotic therapies without success.
O: Medical records indicate a skin biopsy showing dermal-hypodermal neutrophilic infiltrate, suggestive of Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG). Previous treatments included pulse-therapy with intravenous methylprednisolone 125 mg daily for 5 days and a combination of prednisone at tapering dosages with cyclosporine 300 mg daily, resulting in clinical improvement. Additionally, a chest CT and neck ultrasound revealed a multinodular goiter with suspicious nodules in the thyroid. Fine-needle aspiration categorized as Tir4, suspicious for thyroid carcinoma. Total thyroidectomy confirmed multicentric classical Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma (PTC) with focal extension to extra-thyroidal soft tissues. Post-surgical treatment included radioactive iodine ablation with 3700 MBq under Thyrogen® stimulation, showing no uptake outside the thyroid bed and a stimulated Tg serum value of 2.6 ng/ml.
A: The primary diagnosis is Pyoderma Gangrenosum with a secondary diagnosis of multicentric classical Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma (pT3(m)Nx). The patient has responded well to the treatment for both conditions, with no evidence of metastatic disease or recurrence of thyroid cancer post-treatment.
P: Continue monitoring the healing of the skin ulcers and manage any flare-ups of PG with appropriate immunosuppressive therapy. Regular follow-up is necessary to monitor thyroid function and detect any recurrence of thyroid cancer. The patient should maintain regular appointments with both dermatology and endocrinology, and report any new symptoms or concerns immediately. Further education on the signs of PG flare-ups and symptoms of thyroid dysfunction should be provided to ensure prompt reporting and management.

**SOAP Summary:**

**Vital Signs:** Stable

**Subjective Data:**
- Patient reports painful skin ulcers in the mammary region that have been present for about 6 months.
- Ulcers initially appeared as pustular lesions and have progressed rapidly.
- Symptoms include red and purplish edges around the ulcers.
- Tried antiseptic and antibiotic therapies without significant improvement.

**Objective Data:**
- Skin biopsy showed a dermal-hypodermal neutrophilic infiltrate, suggestive of PG.
- Laboratory tests ruled out underlying inflammatory conditions.
- Hospitalized for 5 days with pulse-therapy with intravenous methylprednisolone 125 mg daily, followed by prednisone and cyclosporine.
- Clinical improvement noted with healing of lesions.
- Chest CT scan revealed a multinodular goiter.
- Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the dominant nodule showed suspicious for thyroid carcinoma (Tir4).
- Total thyroidectomy performed, histopathology confirmed multicentric classical PTC with extra-thyroidal extension.
- Post-surgery radioactive iodine ablation treatment with 3700 MBq Thyrogen®.
- Whole Body Scan shows no extrathyroidal uptake, and stimulated Tg serum value is 2.6 ng/ml.

**Assessment:**
- Multicentric classical papillary thyroid carcinoma with extra-thyroidal extension.
- Follow-up required for monitoring and treatment compliance.

**Plan:**
- Regular follow-ups with oncology and endocrinology teams.
- Report any new symptoms or concerns promptly.

Doctor: Hello, I see that you're a 22-year-old man who's been experiencing some symptoms lately. Can you please tell me more about what you've been going through?
Patient: Yeah, I've been having this chronic upper gastrointestinal bleeding for a while now. It's been really bothering me.
Doctor: I see. That sounds quite concerning. We need to perform some tests to figure out what might be causing this bleeding. We'll start with an endoscopy to get a better look at your stomach.
Patient: Okay, sounds good.
(After the endoscopy)
Doctor: Your endoscopy results showed that you have massive gastric polyposis, which could be the cause of your bleeding. We also performed a colonoscopy, which showed a few polyps. We initially tried endoscopic polypectomy to remove the polyps.
Patient: Yeah, I remember that.
Doctor: Unfortunately, your symptoms progressed, and we had to take further action. We decided to perform a total gastrectomy to remove your entire stomach.
Patient: Oh wow, that's a major surgery. I hope it helps with my bleeding issue.
Doctor: Yes, it is a significant procedure, but it was necessary due to the severity of your condition. We sent the tissue samples for histology to confirm the diagnosis.
Patient: And what did the histology results say?
Doctor: The histology results confirmed that you have massive gastric juvenile polyposis. This is the cause of your bleeding, and the total gastrectomy should help manage the symptoms.
Patient: I see. So, what do I need to do now for follow-up care?
Doctor: We'll closely monitor your recovery and schedule regular check-ups to ensure that the issue has been resolved. It's essential to maintain a healthy diet and follow any specific instructions given by your healthcare team.
Patient: Thank you, Doctor. I appreciate your help and guidance during this challenging time.
Doctor: You're welcome. If you have any further concerns or questions, please don't hesitate to reach out. We're here to support you throughout your recovery.

Doctor: Hello, I see that you have been admitted to the emergency service. Can you please tell me about your symptoms?
Patient: Yeah, I've been having hiccups and fever for the past six weeks. The hiccups usually happen when there's some lead stimulation.
Doctor: I see. I understand you have a history of dilated cardiomyopathy and had a dual chamber pacemaker and implantable cardioverter defibrillator implanted due to atrioventricular complete block three months ago, is that correct?
Patient: Yes, that's right. I had a coronary angiography before the procedure, and they said my arteries were normal.
Doctor: Okay, and I also see that you were given antibiotic therapy for a fever six weeks after the implantation. You have a history of diabetes and hypertension for 10 years, correct?
Patient: Yes, that's accurate.
Doctor: When you were admitted, your fever was at 39.5°C, blood pressure at 100/50 mmHg, and heart rate at 100/min. We also noted rough pulmonary sounds, but no crepitation, and a 2/6 pansystolic murmur at all cardiac foci. Have you noticed any swelling in your legs?
Patient: Yeah, there's been some mild edema in both of my legs.
Doctor: We also observed splinter hemorrhages in your nails. Your electrocardiogram showed pacemaker rhythms and a bigeminy ventricular extrasystole. A chest x-ray revealed a bilateral reticulonodular pattern, bilateral pleural effusions, and an increased cardiothoracic index. The pacemaker generator implanted in your left pectoral region was present along with two leads, which appeared to extend into the heart. 
Patient: Oh, I see.
Doctor: Your lab tests showed a WBC of 12,300/m3, sedimentation of 62mm, and CRP of 235 mg/L. Your HbA1C level was 6.2%. With these clinical manifestations, you were hospitalized with the diagnosis of unknown fever and hiccups in the department of infectious diseases. You were started on drug therapy with sulbactam, ampicillin, and ciprofloxacin. 
Patient: Okay, and how did that go?
Doctor: Your blood culture yielded a positive result for methicillin-sensitive staphylococcus aureus. Your body temperature decreased for a while, but it increased again to 39°C on day nine. Consequently, you were designated to undergo a transthoracic echocardiography. Unfortunately, your hiccups have continued throughout this period.
Patient: Oh, I see. So, what's the next step in my treatment?
Doctor: We will continue to monitor your condition and adjust your medications as needed. We'll also perform regular tests to track your progress. It's essential to keep a close eye on your symptoms and report any changes to your medical team.
Patient: Alright, thank you for explaining everything to me, Doctor.
Doctor: You're welcome. If you have any questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out. We're here to help you through this process.
soap:
S: The patient reports persistent hiccups and fever for six weeks, exacerbated by lead stimulation from a pacemaker. The patient has a history of dilated cardiomyopathy, atrioventricular complete block, diabetes, and hypertension. He underwent pacemaker and implantable cardioverter defibrillator implantation three months ago, followed by antibiotic therapy for fever. He also reports mild edema in both legs.
O: On admission, the patient's vital signs were: fever at 39.5°C, blood pressure at 100/50 mmHg, and heart rate at 100/min. Physical examination revealed rough pulmonary sounds, a 2/6 pansystolic murmur at all cardiac foci, and splinter hemorrhages in nails. The chest x-ray showed a bilateral reticulonodular pattern, bilateral pleural effusions, and an increased cardiothoracic index. Lab tests indicated a WBC of 12,300/m3, sedimentation rate of 62mm, CRP of 235 mg/L, and HbA1C of 6.2%. Blood culture was positive for methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus.
A: The primary diagnosis is infectious endocarditis, likely secondary to pacemaker implantation, indicated by persistent fever, positive blood culture, and cardiac and pulmonary findings. Differential diagnoses include pacemaker lead infection and systemic infection secondary to diabetes. The prognosis depends on the response to antibiotic therapy and management of underlying chronic conditions.
P: Continue current antibiotic regimen of sulbactam, ampicillin, and ciprofloxacin. Adjust medications based on ongoing lab and clinical findings. Schedule regular monitoring through transthoracic echocardiography and additional blood cultures to assess treatment efficacy. Educate the patient on signs of worsening infection and the importance of compliance with the treatment plan. Consider consultation with a cardiologist for pacemaker management and an infectious disease specialist for tailored antibiotic therapy.

raw_note:
Doctor: Hello there, how can I help you today?
Patient: Hi, I'm a 22-year-old unmarried South Asian female. I've been experiencing some issues with my right eye for the past four months.
Doctor: I see. Can you describe the issues you've been experiencing?
Patient: Yes, it all started with a headache that lasted one day and then I began to notice metamorphopsia in my right eye. Like, when I look at the straight grid lines on Excel, they appear wavy.
Doctor: Hmm, that's concerning. Have you had any issues with your vision before this?
Patient: Yeah, I've had refractive errors in both my right (-1.00 dioptre sphere, DS) and left (-0.50 DS) eyes for three years now.
Doctor: Okay, let's perform an ocular examination first. *After the examination* I found that your best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) in the right eye is 6/24. Your left eye has a visual acuity of 6/18 and a BCVA of 6/6.
Patient: Is that bad, doctor?
Doctor: It's not ideal, but let's see what else we find. *After the fundus examination* I noticed 2+ vitreous cells and a slightly elevated area of yellow-white active retinitis of two to three disc diameters (DD) over the macula in your right eye.
Patient: What does that mean?
Doctor: It means there's some inflammation and retinitis in your right eye. We'll need to perform a fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) to get a better understanding. *After FFA* The results show a hyperfluorescent patch of increasing intensity of 2DD temporal to the optic disc in your right eye.
Patient: What's the next step?
Doctor: We'll conduct an axial optical coherence tomography (OCT) scan. *After OCT scan* The scan revealed an irregular, thickened surface of the retina with loss of foveal contour and pigment epithelial detachment in your right eye. These findings are highly suggestive of a choroidal neovascular membrane.
Patient: What does that mean for me?
Doctor: It's a condition that can cause vision problems like the ones you've been experiencing. I also performed an optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) and it revealed a patch of abnormally dilated, hyperfluorescent vessels at the level of the outer retina in your right eye.
Patient: Is there any connection to other health issues?
Doctor: We've ruled out clinical manifestations of systemic Toxoplasma infection. There's no history of ocular trauma, comorbid condition, or any significant past medical and surgical history. There's also no history of drug use, allergies, addiction, or blood transfusion. How's your sleep?
Patient: My sleep is normal, doctor.
Doctor: That's good. We'll need to monitor your condition and discuss treatment options to address the choroidal neovascular membrane in your right eye. It's essential to attend follow-up appointments to track your progress and make any necessary adjustments to your treatment plan.
Patient: Alright, thank you for explaining everything, doctor. I'll make sure to attend my follow-up appointments.

S: A 22-year-old unmarried South Asian female presents with a four-month history of metamorphopsia in her right eye, initially preceded by a one-day headache. She reports seeing wavy lines when looking at straight grid lines. The patient has a history of refractive errors in both eyes, with a longer duration in the right eye.
O: Ocular examination reveals a best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) of 6/24 in the right eye and 6/6 in the left eye. Fundus examination shows 2+ vitreous cells and an area of active retinitis of approximately 2-3 disc diameters over the macula in the right eye. Fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) indicates a hyperfluorescent patch of 2DD temporal to the optic disc in the right eye. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) reveals an irregular, thickened retinal surface with loss of foveal contour and pigment epithelial detachment, suggesting a choroidal neovascular membrane. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) shows a patch of abnormally dilated, hyperfluorescent vessels at the level of the outer retina in the right eye.
A: The primary diagnosis is choroidal neovascular membrane in the right eye, likely causing the patient's visual distortions. Differential diagnoses such as systemic Toxoplasma infection have been ruled out based on clinical evaluation and patient history.
P: The management plan includes close monitoring of the patient's condition with regular follow-up appointments to assess the progression of the choroidal neovascular membrane and adjust treatment as necessary. Discussion of treatment options, possibly including pharmacological interventions, will be conducted during follow-ups. The patient is advised to maintain her follow-up schedule diligently to manage her condition effectively.

**SOAP Summary:**

- **Subjective:**
  - Patient is a 22-year-old unmarried South Asian female with a history of refractive errors in both eyes for 3 years (right eye -1.00 DS, left eye -0.50 DS).
  - She has experienced a headache for 1 day followed by metamorphopsia in her right eye, which appears as wavy straight lines on an Excel grid.
  - No prior history of vision issues.

- **Objective:**
  - Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA): Right eye 6/24, Left eye 6/18.
  - Fundus examination: 2+ vitreous cells, slightly elevated area of yellow-white active retinitis of 2-3 disc diameters (DD) over the macula.
  - Fluorescein Angiography (FFA): Hyperfluorescent patch of increasing intensity of 2DD temporal to the optic disc.
  - Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT): Irregular, thickened surface of the retina with loss of foveal contour and pigment epithelial detachment.
  - Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA): Patch of abnormally dilated, hyperfluorescent vessels at the level of the outer retina.

- **Assessment:**
  - Choroidal neovascular membrane in the right eye.
  - Possible systemic Toxoplasma infection ruled out.

- **Plan:**
  - Follow-up appointments every 3 months to monitor progression and adjust treatment plan.
  - Treatment options may include anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) therapy, corticosteroids, or photodynamic therapy.
  - Monitor sleep patterns and overall health status.

Doctor: Hello, I see you've been referred here because of a 20-year history of intermittent headaches and dizziness, accompanied by blurred vision in both eyes for the past 10 years. Is that correct?
Patient: Yes, that's right.
Doctor: Do you have any family history of glaucoma?
Patient: No, I don't.
Doctor: Alright. We performed an ophthalmic examination, which showed that your best corrected visual acuity values were 0.8 and 0.5 in your eyes. The intraocular pressure was normal, but the superior visual field was narrowed in both eyes, likely due to drooping upper eyelids.
Patient: Oh, I see.
Doctor: We also conducted a neurological examination. Your muscle strength and muscle tension in the limbs were normal, and Babinski's sign, Kernig's sign, and Brudzinski's sign were negative.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: A CT scan and an MRI of the sella revealed a mass in the sellar-suprasellar-parasellar region. This mass is likely to be a meningioma, which compressed your left optic nerve.
Patient: Oh no, that sounds serious.
Doctor: Yes, it is a serious condition. We performed a craniotomy using the anterior cranial base approach. During the operation, we opened the frontal sinus apex and found the tumor surrounding your left optic nerve, close to the internal carotid artery and the oculomotor nerve.
Patient: Was the surgery successful?
Doctor: Yes, the operation was successful. We removed as much of the tumor as possible, sealed the top of the frontal sinus with bone wax, and sutured the epidural.
Patient: That's a relief.
Doctor: A postoperative CT showed that the lesion in the saddle area had been removed, and a small amount of blood and effusion accumulated under the dural membrane of the left frontotemporal region.
Patient: Oh, okay.
Doctor: On the third day after the operation, you complained of swelling and pain in your left eye, accompanied by difficulty opening the left eyelid. Is that right?
Patient: Yes, it's been very uncomfortable.
Doctor: Our examination revealed proptosis with ptosis in the left eye, eyelid swelling, and increases in intraorbital pressure and intraocular pressure.
Patient: What can be done about this?
Doctor: We will monitor your condition closely and provide appropriate treatment to manage the swelling, pain, and pressure. It's important to follow up with us regularly to ensure your recovery goes smoothly.
Patient: Alright, thank you, doctor.

O: Ophthalmic examination revealed best corrected visual acuity of 0.8 and 0.5 in the eyes, normal intraocular pressure, and narrowed superior visual fields due to drooping upper eyelids. Neurological examination showed normal muscle strength and tension, with negative Babinski's, Kernig's, and Brudzinski's signs. CT and MRI identified a mass in the sellar-suprasellar-parasellar region compressing the left optic nerve. Post-craniotomy CT confirmed removal of the lesion with residual blood and effusion under the dural membrane of the left frontotemporal region. Recent examination indicated proptosis with ptosis in the left eye, eyelid swelling, and increased intraorbital and intraocular pressures.
A: Primary diagnosis is a meningioma compressing the left optic nerve, with postoperative complications including proptosis, ptosis, and increased intraorbital and intraocular pressures. Differential diagnosis could include other causes of increased intraorbital pressure.
P: Continue close monitoring of the patient's postoperative condition. Manage swelling, pain, and increased pressures with appropriate medications and interventions. Regular follow-ups are essential to assess recovery and manage any ongoing or new symptoms. Educate the patient on signs of complications and the importance of adherence to follow-up appointments and treatment plans.

raw_note:
Doctor: Hello, I see that you were hospitalized in July 2012. Can you tell me more about the symptoms you experienced at that time?
Patient: Yes, I had edema in my left lower extremities and a painful inguinal mass for about 6 months.
Doctor: I see. Upon your physical examination, we found matted bilateral inguinal lymph nodes up to 9 cm in size with hard consistency, erythema, and tenderness without fluctuation or ulcer. We also noticed marked swelling at both lower extremities. There was no other superficial lymphadenopathy, and your other physical examinations were normal.
Patient: Hmm, okay.
Doctor: We performed an incisional biopsy of the left inguinal lymph node, which revealed a metastatic round cell tumor. The immunohistochemistry was positive for vimentin, S100, and HMB-45. The immunophenotype is consistent with malignant melanoma.
Patient: Oh no, that's not good.
Doctor: We suspected primary tumors in the lower extremities, abdominal cavity, and anogenital organ. We conducted a complete skin examination, ophthalmoscopy, gastroscopy, colonoscopy, and cystoscopy, but no cutaneous lesion was identified. Your genital and pelvic examinations also did not show any evidence of a lesion.
Patient: So you couldn't find the primary source?
Doctor: That's correct. You were diagnosed with metastatic melanoma of unknown primary. A CT scan of your abdomen showed multiple enlarged lymph nodes throughout the abdominal and pelvic cavity up to 9.5 cm, along with compression of both iliac veins without an organ-specific lesion. Your CT chest was unremarkable.
Patient: I remember having a lot of pain during that time.
Doctor: Yes, you developed severe pain requiring high-dose opioid treatment. You then underwent 20 Gy of palliative radiotherapy for bilateral inguinal lymph nodes. Despite the radiotherapy, the remaining tumors were up to 7.4 cm based on the CT scan.
Patient: What about further treatment?
Doctor: Unfortunately, due to your reimbursement issue and national policy, you couldn't access an immune checkpoint inhibitor or targeted drug. Instead, chemotherapy was prescribed with carboplatin (AUC5) and paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 for 6 cycles.
Patient: Did the chemotherapy help?
Doctor: After completing the planned chemotherapy, your symptom was slightly improved. The CT scan at the first 3 months showed a response to the treatment. So, we will continue to monitor your condition and provide appropriate follow-up care.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I appreciate all the help.
